text: Is it showing up? Oh, no. Can everyone hear me at least? Okay, good. All right. Wow, I was not
expecting this many people at the end of a conference, but we're gonna roll with it. I'm
still recovering from last night, so we're gonna do our best. It's Nick's fault, actually, so you
can blame him. So today I wanted to talk about at least my year building Bridgie and breaking down
the walls of the open social web. My co-founder, Ryan Barrett, has been doing it much longer than
I have. But, yeah, please clap. Please clap for Ryan Barrett. He's an amazing person.
But, yeah, it's been a year for me, and over the last year, I think we've built a lot, but
more importantly, I think through that building, I've learned a lot more about, not just about
the ecosystems and how they operate and what we need to start caring about, but about the
way that we can start building as well. And so I wanted to share some of those findings.
A little introduction to myself. I'm Anuja Huja. I'm the Executive Director at AnuSocial.
We are, our goal is to liberate people's online relationships from any one technology. We're
a non-profit, and we've been operating for about a month and, a year and change, a month.
That was last year. I also write on my blog, Augment, where I'm actually experimenting with
a bunch of open social web tools as well. But today I wanted to focus on the work that
we've been doing at AnuSocial. And I always like to start these presentations with the
statement that I remind myself of. And it's a promise that I make my friends, my family,
and my colleagues, whenever I introduce them to the idea of the open social web. And it's
this concept of, "You can follow anyone from anywhere in Liefer, another platform at any
time." And I think the only thing that bothers me about this statement is the fact that I
always have to add the caveat of "kinda." And we all know why. I'm not going to get into
some reasons. But to me, the fact that there's a "kinda" really sucks. And so the thing that
we're trying to do at AnuSocial is trying to make that "kinda" smaller and smaller and
smaller over time until it no longer exists. So that's really the basis of the work that
we're trying to do. I introduced a version of that idea back last year at Atmosphere Conference,
where I had my presentation, Bridges and the Last Network Effect. I have a blog post about
it. I'm not going to recap too much about that presentation in particular. But at the time
when I did that presentation, AnuSocial was like a new foundation. We just started and a
lot of the stuff that I was saying on stage were really just like dreams. It was more of
a multi-year vision. And quite frankly, I actually didn't think most of it was possible. But it
turned out that it wasn't a multi-year vision because with Ryan Barrett, I was able to do
it in a single year. We actually ended up building most of the stuff that I talked about there,
or at least the foundational layers of what we had to build. And so today I wanted to go
through some of the highlights of that year and specifically talk about some of the lessons
I learned while we were doing it. So let's talk about 2025. Our biggest launch of the year was
probably Bounce. It was an eject button for the OpenSocial Web where you could actually migrate
your social graph between protocols, keeping all of your followers and as many bridged follows as possible.
This ended up being way bigger than I thought. I thought a bunch of protocol nerds would get excited.
It turned out everyone was excited. And we got a bunch of users to try it out for the first time.
People are reviewing their social graphs on there all the time. We thought it was just like a neat
UI trick. Turns out people really care about their social graphs and finding out how multi-protocol they
are. Very surprising. I did not expect that at all. But what I learned was we could actually do a lot
more with the bridge than I originally thought. And that was like the biggest lesson for me was
this bridge is more than just connecting communication. We could actually start doing a lot more if we use
it as the foundation for other products. We also launched a settings page. We launched a bunch of
features for that last year. So I don't want to go into each thing. But the one thing that I wanted to call
out that I was really excited about was launching app proto block lists on BridgeyFed which meant that
Fediverse users could take advantage of an atmosphere feature. And it was the first time where it clicked
for me where I was like we're not just bridging profiles and posts. We can actually bridge full-blown
features across ecosystems using BridgeyFed. And like huh, that like really unlocked something in my brain.
And I haven't been able to stop thinking about it since we launched it. The other thing that we
launched was unbridge notifications which if someone was on the other side of the bridge and they
communicated with you but they weren't bridged themselves, we couldn't show it on your native
platform, that sucks. There's no visibility of trolls or like people that you actually care about
interacting with you. And so we gave you some visibility into that over notifications, over your
DMs. That was a really neat trick that Ryan put together for us. And then finally we started seeing a
bunch of platforms just putting bridging as a part of their platforms, which was really cool. One that
I want to call out is AltStore. They're, yeah, clap for AltStore. They're freaking amazing. They're here by
the way. You should talk to them. They're awesome. And the cool thing about AltStore is they're also
breaking down walls, specifically the walled garden of Apple which should have been broken down a long
time ago. But that's a whole other story. We also learned something much bigger this year, at least to me.
It was that Bridgifed actually hosts some of the most active users on either network. We have about 150,000
accounts on Bridgifed now. And they are some of the most power user people and they don't care about
who they're talking to and where they are. They just want to build a community. And all they care
about is connecting with people regardless of the platform they're on. That was just a statement I made
last year. Turns out like a lot of people agree with that. And they're some of the most active users
everywhere. With Bounce as well, I recently learned that we're doing about six to eight migrations every
month, which is a whole lot more than I expected for protocol migration. I know that's small numbers
when you compare it to the crazy numbers that folks are doing on PDS Mover and Black Sky. But protocol
movement's a lot more complicated and a lot more scary. And we're still in beta. And most people don't
even know we exist. And so eight to ten migrations a month -- sorry, six to eight migrations a month
kind of blew my mind. But the thing is like these numbers don't really mean a lot to me. I don't
really look -- like Ryan will tell you this. I don't look at DAOs. I don't look at MAUs. I don't really
look at the activity that's happening on this stuff. In fact, I poked Ryan for these numbers like last
minute because I was like I should probably say some of these numbers while I'm on stage as like the executive
director. But like to me, this stuff doesn't really matter. And I think Lawrence Hoff of Connected Spaces wrote it
better than I could ever say it. He said that tools like Bounce suggest the direction of the open social
web is less about specific protocols and more about expanding user agency. And I think he kind of nailed
it. The way that we say it, which I think is less creative, is we just think these things should exist
and that people need them. And so we build them because they need them. And that's really it.
Maybe five users are going to use it. Maybe 150,000 people are going to use it. But to us, these things need
to exist because people care about connecting with people and using the tools that work best for them.
And so that's why we build the things that we build. Or as David Pierce on The Verge said about Bounce,
the idea that I can just pick up my stuff on a social network and put it down on another social network,
like that's it, baby. That's the stuff.
And so this year we're going to keep doing that stuff because clearly that stuff matters and we really
want to keep doing that work. And so we've already hit the ground running with 2026. It's only been
three months and we've actually launched a bunch of stuff. I want to quickly go over some of those
things. For one, we launched the Fediverse version of block lists for atmosphere users. So now we've
bridged a feature from the Fediverse over to the atmosphere. And we've actually given people some
options from reputable sources who give you full-blown block lists right away. So you don't even have to
think about it. You just go to our page, you click a few block lists, and then you don't have to worry
about some of the worst trolls on the Fediverse. We also improved unbridge notifications with a full UI.
So when people interact with you and they can't bridge themselves, and they're not bridging themselves,
sorry, you can just go to the BridgeFed UI. You can like, repost, block, or reply to them.
And to me, this was like another unlock because I realized that, hey, we can actually just do one-way
interactions once in a while and it's not that big of a deal. And we also did it in a way where
it preserves the privacy of the other user. Like if they don't want to be on the atmosphere or the
Fediverse, they don't have to be. They can still interact with people on either side. And I think that that's
really important as well. And the biggest, like most exciting thing that's happened so far this year,
at least for me, was long form bridging. We're now bridging standard.site, connecting blogs across
the atmosphere, the Fediverse, and the web all at once. It's really, really exciting to me. And I want
to give a huge shout out to Leaflet Pocket and Offprint, who not only created standard.site,
but also helped us a lot in the process of us building standard.site into BridgeFed. I'm really
excited to see people start using this. I'm already using it on my blog, and I've already heard from a few
people who've started using it as well. So I'm super excited about this. I also want to take an
opportunity to apologize to Jared. I don't think he's in the room, but Jared was at a conference in
Europe, and he was about to promote Leaflet and standard.site, and then we exploded their app view.
Like overnight, because we didn't tell him that we were doing this, and we just turned on our fire hose,
and he was like, "Dude, what's happening with my app view?" He messaged me, I was like, "I don't think we did
anything." And Ryan's like, "Yeah, we did." So yeah, I just wanted to publicly apologize to Jared,
but we can just do things. So yeah, we did it. But yeah, so that's what we've done so much so far
with BridgeFed. But one of the neat things we did with the standard.site integration is we actually
made it way easier to add other lexicons to BridgeFed to bridge to the Fediverse. And so if you're
building a new product with its own lexicon, and you think it deserves more distribution across these
ecosystems across the web, across the Fediverse, please reach out to us. We've actually made it
like dead easy. And we'd love to work with more people, and I think the distribution is really
important to the users that are on your platforms. So we'd love to work with you. If you look at our
GitHub, you might notice that we've also been playing around with some other protocols. Not going to talk
too much about that today, but you can probably take a look and find out within a couple of minutes.
Hopefully something to announce later this year. So that's BridgeFed. Then there's Bounce. Bounce was a
really exciting project, as I said before. But one of the limitations that we had in the original
version of Bounce is you actually had to have an existing account at least on one side. So if you
wanted to move from the Fediverse to the Atmosphere, you had to have an Atmosphere account. If you wanted
to move from the Atmosphere to the Fediverse, you had to have a Fediverse account already. With the
Fediverse, that doesn't matter as much because you can merge accounts. So if you had an Atmosphere account
that was bridged, we just merged the bridged version of your account in. But the other way is not possible. You
cannot merge Atmosphere repos. And so we'd have to actually drop one of your accounts. And oftentimes
that was the bridged version of the account. So we actually had some users that migrated from the
Atmosphere to the Fediverse and they wanted to go back. And we had no way to send them back. And it
really, really sucked. But the reason we didn't launch with that was, for one, Blue Sky wasn't allowing
migrations back into their PDSs at that time. They do now. So that's changed. And the other thing that's
changed since then is that there's just a lot more PDS providers, which is like just really exciting.
Kudos to everyone running their own PDSs, people running PDS migration services. We've taken a lot
of inspiration from you guys. And so I'm really excited to announce that in the next couple of weeks,
we're actually going to launch full migrations from the Fediverse to the Atmosphere, to any PDS that
you want, whether that's Black Sky, Euro Sky, your own self-hosted of PDS or self-hosted social. It's your
choice. You get to choose the PDS where you go and you can migrate all of your Fediverse followers in
as you go. Thank you.
Yeah, personally, very excited. I really wanted to launch with that. But yeah,
limitations were there. A cool byproduct of that, though, is that because we're able to
move people to PDSs and Fediverse accounts and merge Fediverse accounts, we can actually give people
a credible exit from BridgyFed itself. There's a lot of other bridges and multi-protocol services out there
now. And they also have tools like Bounce that are fully integrated into them. And so we want to give
you the opportunity to be able to leave BridgyFed if one of those other services or platforms actually
serves you better. And so now you can actually split your BridgyFed account in half, create two native
accounts, and then do what you will from there. It's your choice. It's your accounts. It's your life.
So we're really excited to give people, finally, a credible exit from BridgyFed. We don't want to
be the only people here. We don't want to be centralized. We want as many bridges out there as possible.
So we're really excited to be able to give people that opportunity. The other thing that we're going
to be launching later this year is being able to reconnect with unbridged follows. So when you
bounce, we can bring all of your followers with you. But anyone that isn't bridged, who you used to
follow, we can't, you can't follow them. They're not bridged. They don't show up in your native account.
So one of the things we're going to do to circumvent that is actually keep stock of all the users,
that all of the people that you can no longer follow. And if they ever bridge that account in the future,
we give you a notification so that you can immediately reconnect with them when they
arrive natively to your platform. So we're getting closer. You can follow anyone from anywhere and
leave for another platform at any time. And it's still kind of, but I think in the last year,
we've done a pretty great job of making that kind of a whole lot smaller. There's also a whole bunch
of APIs that we launched that I didn't get the chance to talk about here, that you can use a lot of these
features natively in your clients. So if you have a client and you're building on the social web,
please reach out to us. You can actually make a lot of this stuff feel a lot more native
and make that kind of even smaller on your platform. So please reach out to us for that.
Before I leave though, I know I'm running out of time. Sorry, I'm so sorry. I had so much to say.
I had a bunch of dreams that I said last year that we accomplished.
But if there's one thing that I've learned in the last year is that I need to dream a little bit bigger
because Ryan is just insane. He can build literally anything I throw at him. So I'm going to dream way bigger.
I didn't even tell Ryan I'm going to say this, by the way. Paul Frazee said something really interesting
yesterday in his presentation. He said that one of the biggest benefits of the atmosphere
is that you have a single account that works across multiple different services and shares data across
all of those services. I've been calling that the everything account. I'm not the only one that's been
calling it everything. I think Dame has been calling it the everything ecosystem. So like I'm not the only one saying this,
but I've been using the everything account because that's what's been making sense to me and some other folks.
But the everything account isn't like an everything account. It's an everything account for the atmosphere.
And with Bridgifed, if there's one thing that I've realized over the course of the last year is
it can actually help make the atmosphere account and everything account for everything. And so my dream
over the next few years, maybe it's going to be multi-year or maybe Ryan's going to push some code tonight.
But my dream for the future is actually to make your atmosphere account, your everything account for
everything. And so that's the mission that I'm on for the next few years with Ryan and the rest of
the Anu Social team. So thank you guys very much. I'm sorry for going over.
We are a non-profit with two full-time volunteers for the last year. So if you believe in our mission,
please go to our Patreon, patreon.com/anusocial. You can get People's Not Platform merch on
store.anu.social. And you can find me all over the atmosphere. Thank you guys so much. And I'm
really sorry for taking away too much.
